blurb: A silver-stemmed native shrub that erupts in clouds of golden flowers in fall when little else blooms — a critical late-season pollinator plant, bone-dry tough and deer-proof.
seasons: Silvery narrow foliage; masses of golden flowers late summer–fall; fluffy tan seedheads into winter
wildlife: Critical late-season nectar for bees and butterflies (incl. migrating monarchs); seeds for birds

care.hardiness: Iron-hardy in cold, dry country — comfortably through Boulder's zone 5b–6a (rated to about zone 4) and native to plains and foothills receiving only 7–18 in of precipitation.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring or fall on a hot, sharply drained, unirrigated site; it establishes fast from container stock. In pots it's less cold-hardy and resents wet feet, so use gritty mix, water sparingly, and shelter over winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — it demands all-day Front Range sun and open, dry exposure.[1][2]
care.soil: Grows on lean loamy, sandy, gravelly, or even heavy clay soils that may be slightly acidic to strongly alkaline or saline; the essential is sharp drainage.[1]
care.water: Very drought tolerant; once established it needs essentially no supplemental water in our semi-arid climate and rots in over-watered or poorly drained ground.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grows readily from its abundant wind-borne seed (non-dormant, though a short cold-moist stratification speeds and broadens germination); it also resprouts vigorously from basal buds and tolerates heavy pruning in production.[1]
care.bloom: Domed clusters of bright golden-yellow flowers cover the silvery-green foliage in late summer into fall (roughly late July–October), a key late-season nectar source; the seed heads persist for winter interest.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low care; it resprouts readily from the base and self-sows, so deadheading spent flowers helps limit volunteer seedlings.[1]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free; some plants get harmless stem galls from tephritid flies, and the main risk is root rot in wet, heavy soil.[1]
care.pruning: Cut it back hard in early spring, before new growth, taking the whole shrub down to roughly 6–12 in; it flowers late in the season on new wood, so a spring cut sacrifices no bloom and it resprouts vigorously from the base. This annual hard prune is what keeps it dense and compact — left unpruned it grows leggy and top-heavy and the woody crown splits open and flops. Avoid cutting hard in late fall, which can leave the fresh cuts winter-damaged.[1][2]

references.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guide — Rubber Rabbitbrush (Ericameria nauseosa)
Plant Select — Baby Blue Rabbitbrush (Ericameria nauseosa)

edible.summary: Not a food plant — a resinous, latex-bearing shrub grown for late-season bloom and wildlife, with no edible food use.[1]
edible.caution: Rabbitbrush contains rubbery latex and resins, is unpalatable, and has occasionally been reported toxic to livestock; it is not eaten by people. Native peoples chewed a gum or brewed twig teas from it medicinally, but that is not a documented food use — don't eat it.[1]
